title: Prof Detlef van Vuuren: The climate scientist most cited by the IPCC
gist: Prof Detlef van Vuuren, the most-cited scientist across all IPCC reports, argues that the retirement of the RCP8.5 high-emission scenario does not mean climate risk has diminished — the world remains on the medium emission pathway and by 2150 even the new high-emission scenario reaches equivalent forcing levels. He warns that achieving the Paris 1.5C target now requires on the order of 500 GtCO2 of carbon removal, warns that IAMs underrepresent tipping points, and flags China's energy transition choices and data centre electricity demand as critical unmodelled variables. He also warns that AI-generated content is already passing peer review in climate journals.

themes.theme: RCP8.5 retirement — what it does and does not mean

insights.claim: Prof van Vuuren states that the RCP8.5 high-emission scenario is no longer plausible as originally constructed because global emissions have tracked the medium pathway since 2010, but the temperature levels it implied could still occur due to climate sensitivity uncertainty and carbon cycle feedbacks.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: RCP8.5's retirement as an emission scenario does not reduce tail risk — high-temperature outcomes remain possible via high climate sensitivity, a critical distinction for long-horizon investors and insurers.
insights.quote: No…because that also depends on climate sensitivity. It also depends on carbon-cycle feedbacks and we still have to research that.

insights.claim: The new high-emission scenario replacing RCP8.5 will, by 2150, lead to similar radiative forcing levels as the original RCP8.5 — the danger is merely delayed by roughly 50 years, not eliminated.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Investors with century-scale horizons (infrastructure, real estate, insurance) cannot treat RCP8.5 retirement as risk reduction — the forcing trajectory is delayed, not avoided.
insights.quote: By 2150, actually, [it] will lead to similar forcing levels as the old RCP8.5. It's simply delayed, but it could still happen.

insights.claim: Trump's social-media post claiming RCP8.5 is 'wrong, wrong, wrong' was politically motivated by fossil-fuel interests seeking to delegitimise climate modelling, not by scientific evidence, according to van Vuuren.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Political attacks on climate models create near-term narrative risk for clean energy policy and may provide justification for regulatory rollbacks — a market risk to monitor.
insights.quote: The fact that we now are not considering that high-emission scenario anymore was an easy target for those people that have an interest in no climate policy.

insights.claim: Van Vuuren says the world in 2026 is 'in a worse situation than in 2010' because it followed the medium emission pathway (not the worst case), and reaching Paris goals is now much harder, with close to 1.5C of warming already locked in.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: The medium emission pathway has proven stubbornly durable — 'not following the worst case' is not progress, it is trajectory confirmation. Carbon markets and transition strategies should price this accordingly.
insights.quote: The reality is that, now in 202[6], we're in a worse situation than we were in 2010. We didn't follow that very worst-case scenario, but we did follow that scenario that was following the increase in emissions.

themes.theme: Carbon dioxide removal — scale of need and IAM implications

insights.claim: Van Vuuren calculates that even under the most optimistic mitigation scenario (stabilise emissions by 2025, reach global net-zero in 2050), the world would have emitted approximately 600 GtCO2 above the 1.5C carbon budget, requiring a minimum of ~500 GtCO2 of removal before 2100.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Planetary-scale CDR is not a theoretical backstop — it is a mathematical necessity for any 1.5C pathway, implying massive demand for BECCS, DAC and other removal technologies by mid-century.
insights.quote: we would already be in an overshoot case of 500GtCO2 that we would be above the 1.5C target. So, if we want to come back, somehow we have to remove an amount of CO2 which is in that order of magnitude

insights.claim: Current annual global CO2 emissions are approximately 40 GtCO2 per year, and achieving 1.5C even with CDR would require at least 43% emissions reduction by 2030 — a target the world is 'not even close to'.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: The 2030 emissions gap is already unbridgeable for 1.5C — investors must price in CDR demand growth and a prolonged temperature overshoot rather than a clean 1.5C compliance scenario.
insights.quote: we were indicating for 1.5C you need to be at 43% emission reduction already in 2030, even if you allow for negative emissions. We're not even close to that.

insights.claim: Van Vuuren argues that integrated assessment models (IAMs) are not responsible for policy inaction — the 2030 43% reduction target was clearly communicated — and that CDR was incorporated in scenarios precisely to show feasibility, not to excuse delay.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: The 'CDR as moral hazard' critique is largely unfounded per leading modellers — the bottleneck is political will, not model design. CDR investment is necessary regardless.
insights.quote: It's not the fault of the models that we need quite a large amount of negative emissions to achieve the Paris goals, it's the fault of our inaction.

themes.theme: IAM limitations — tipping points, equity and tipping point underrepresentation

insights.claim: Integrated assessment models (IAMs) are significantly better at modelling smooth emission trajectories than real tipping points, and do not adequately describe actor interests or social tipping dynamics, according to van Vuuren.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Policy and investment decisions based solely on IAM outputs systematically underweight tail-risk events — physical climate risk models require tipping point supplements to be investment-grade.
insights.quote: Yes, I think one of the major things that these models don't describe is some of the possible tipping points that could happen…Overall, they're much better at looking into smooth trajectories than they are considering real tipping points.

insights.claim: IAMs have historically focused on least-cost mitigation pathways, leaving equity and burden-sharing questions underexplored — a gap the research community is now actively correcting, partly in response to policymaker demand.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Growing emphasis on equity in climate scenarios will shift policy design toward different regional mitigation burdens — a forward risk for developing market investors as emissions reduction obligations become more distributional.
insights.quote: A major assumption behind the modelling that was done, let's say, five years ago was we do look into a least-cost pathway. We don't say anything about who is actually paying for it.

themes.theme: China's energy choices as global swing factor

insights.claim: Van Vuuren states that China accounts for roughly one-third of global CO2 emissions and its technology exports in solar PV, wind turbines, EVs and batteries will materially shape energy transition outcomes in other countries.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: China is both the world's largest emitter and the dominant supplier of clean energy technology — its domestic policy choices create global supply chain dependencies that dwarf any single Western country's climate policy.
insights.quote: I think China has, first of all, a major role itself in determining emission trends, with a third of the emissions more or less globally coming from China, but also it has a major impact on emission trends outside of China.

insights.claim: Van Vuuren describes China as pivoting to become an 'electrostate' with cheap EVs and an epic renewables rollout, while the world's largest wind turbine company is also in China.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: China's clean-tech manufacturing dominance means global decarbonisation speed is partly a function of geopolitical access to Chinese supply chains — a critical variable for energy transition timelines.
insights.quote: the world's largest annual emitter China pivoting to becoming an electrostate, with its cheap EVs and epic rollout of renewables…the largest windmill company in the world is in China

themes.theme: Data centres and AI as unmodelled energy demand

insights.claim: Van Vuuren's IMAGE model is now incorporating data centre electricity demand as an additional load component; he estimates the impact as 'a couple of percentage points of additional emissions' — not a doubling — but significant enough to require explicit modelling.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Data centre electricity demand growth is being formally integrated into leading climate models — its scale (~2-3% of global emissions) is material but not scenario-breaking at current trajectory.
insights.quote: in IMAGE what we are developing now is part of the datacentres that is an additional form of electricity use…it's a couple of percentage [points of] additional emissions that we now see happening

insights.claim: Van Vuuren identifies rapid data centre build-out as a 'very negative example' of a trend that moved faster than modellers anticipated — analogous to the (positive) surprise of renewable cost declines — and states current models are catching up.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: IAMs lagging fast-moving trends (data centres, AI power demand) means near-term scenario outputs may systematically underestimate grid stress in the 2025-2030 window.
insights.quote: This [datacentres] is a very negative example. We are looking into this now.

themes.theme: AI disruption of academic peer review

insights.claim: Van Vuuren, as an editor of the journal Climatic Change, confirms he has seen AI-generated articles published in peer-reviewed climate journals, with errors introduced by over-reliance on AI still occasionally passing through review.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: AI contamination of peer-reviewed literature is confirmed and already occurring — a systemic risk to the reliability of the scientific evidence base on which climate risk models are built.
insights.quote: There are articles published that are created by AI and you see errors being created, because people have to rely too much on AI…Sometimes [they get through peer review].

insights.claim: Van Vuuren warns that the volume of grant proposals has increased significantly because AI tools allow faster writing, creating a challenge for science disciplines in determining what AI use is permissible in articles, reviews and proposals.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: AI-inflated research proposal volume will increase cost and decrease selectivity in scientific funding allocation — a longer-term risk to research quality in fields with finite review capacity.
insights.quote: we see in proposal writing that there is now much more proposals being submitted than in the past, as people can use AI tools

insights.claim: The Urgenda court case in the Netherlands saw a judge use an IPCC report — accepted by the Dutch government — to legally compel the government to meet specific emission reduction targets, demonstrating that IPCC reports can carry binding legal weight in domestic courts.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: IPCC reports are increasingly being treated as legally binding benchmarks in domestic litigation — a direct financial risk to governments and companies that have made climate commitments but failed to implement them.
insights.quote: the judge looked at that government, it said: 'You have a constitution where it says that you have to protect your citizens and, according to this IPCC report that you have signed yourself…I hereby rule that is now your target.'

source_meta.author: Leo Hickman, Joe Goodman, Tom Prater
